title: The Tight Chest at the Bus Stop
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on recognition and management of acute asthma exacerbation. Students must recognize respiratory distress, initiate oxygen appropriately, administer bronchodilator therapy, and communicate effectively with a patient who is anxious and struggling to breathe.

callInformation.dispatch: Difficulty breathing at a bus stop

incidentNarrative: The patient was waiting for the bus when she began experiencing increasing shortness of breath and chest tightness. She used her inhaler once with minimal relief. Bystanders called 911 when she began having difficulty speaking in full sentences.
patientPresentation: Patient is sitting upright, visibly anxious, speaking in short phrases, using accessory muscles to breathe. Audible wheezing present. Skin slightly pale but warm and dry. *(💡yellow|Short phrases + accessory muscle use should immediately signal moderate to severe respiratory distress)*

sample.symptoms: Shortness of breath, chest tightness, wheezing
sample.events: Waiting for bus when symptoms started

caseProgression.withTreatment: After oxygen and salbutamol administration, wheezing decreases, respiratory rate improves, patient able to speak full sentences, SpO₂ improves to mid-90s.
caseProgression.withoutTreatment: Respiratory distress worsens, patient becomes fatigued, wheezing decreases with poor air movement, SpO₂ drops into high 80s, patient becomes drowsy.

expectedTreatment: Assist with salbutamol MDI or administer bronchodilator
Monitor vitals and reassess after treatment

clinicalReasoning.mostLikelyDiagnosis: Acute asthma exacerbation due to bronchospasm and airway inflammation causing airflow limitation.

3: Recognizes breathing problem but underestimates severity
5: Recognizes respiratory distress and prioritizes appropriately
7: Immediately recognizes severity of respiratory distress
Excellent clinical reasoning and transport planning

selfReflection: What clinical signs indicated respiratory distress?
How did treatment change the patient condition?
What would you do differently next time?
What complications could occur in severe asthma?

title: The Quiet Diabetic
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on recognition and management of hypoglycemia. Students must identify altered mental status due to low blood glucose and administer appropriate treatment such as oral glucose or glucagon.
incidentNarrative: Family states patient is diabetic and was found confused and diaphoretic this morning. He did not eat breakfast and took his insulin.
patientPresentation: Patient is confused, pale, diaphoretic, slow to respond to questions. *(💡yellow|Altered LOC + diaphoresis in diabetic patient should immediately trigger BGL check)*

sample.events: Took insulin but did not eat

caseProgression.withTreatment: After glucagon or oral glucose, patient becomes alert, diaphoresis resolves, vitals stabilize, patient able to answer questions.
caseProgression.withoutTreatment: Patient LOC decreases, may become unconscious, risk of seizure, worsening hypoglycemia.

expectedTreatment: Administer glucagon if unable to swallow

clinicalReasoning.mostLikelyDiagnosis: Hypoglycemia due to insulin administration without food intake.

selfReflection: Why is checking BGL important in altered LOC?
What complications can occur if untreated?
How did treatment affect the patient?
